expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of the structural anatomy of the heart—the central organ of the cardiovascular system—detailing the four chambers (right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle), the four valves (tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral, aortic), the pathway of blood flow through the heart and great vessels, the cardiac skeleton (fibrous rings supporting valves and providing electrical insulation), and the layers of the heart wall (epicardium, myocardium, endocardium). Emphasis is placed on the structural adaptations for function (right vs. left ventricular differences, valve mechanics, myocardial architecture), the relationship between structure and blood flow, and clinical correlations (valvular heart disease, septal defects, heart failure, myocardial infarction).

core_concept.principle: The heart is a four-chambered dual pump: the right heart (atrium and ventricle) pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs via the pulmonary circulation; the left heart (atrium and ventricle) pumps oxygenated blood to the systemic circulation—with four valves ensuring unidirectional flow, and the myocardium arranged in spiraling layers enabling efficient contraction.
core_concept.explanation:
The heart is a muscular organ approximately the size of a fist, located in the mediastinum, with its apex directed downward and leftward. It functions as two synchronized pumps in series: the right heart receives deoxygenated blood from the systemic veins and pumps it to the lungs; the left heart receives oxygenated blood from the pulmonary veins and pumps it to the systemic arteries.

Four Chambers:
Right Atrium (RA): Receives deoxygenated blood from the superior vena cava (SVC), inferior vena cava (IVC), and coronary sinus. Contains the sinoatrial (SA) node (pacemaker) at the junction with SVC.
Right Ventricle (RV): Pumps deoxygenated blood through the pulmonary valve into the pulmonary trunk. Thinner-walled than left ventricle (pulmonary circulation is low-resistance). Contains trabeculae carneae, papillary muscles, and chordae tendineae that anchor the tricuspid valve.
Left Atrium (LA): Receives oxygenated blood from four pulmonary veins. Forms most of the base of the heart.
Left Ventricle (LV): Pumps oxygenated blood through the aortic valve into the aorta. Thick-walled (3× thicker than RV) to generate high pressure for systemic circulation. Contains papillary muscles and chordae tendineae anchoring the mitral valve.

Four Valves:
Atrioventricular (AV) Valves: Tricuspid (right, three leaflets) and Mitral (left, two leaflets). Prevent backflow into atria during ventricular systole. Papillary muscles contract with ventricles, pulling chordae tendineae to prevent valve prolapse.
Semilunar Valves: Pulmonary (right) and Aortic (left). Each has three cusps (leaflets). Open during ventricular systole, close during diastole to prevent backflow into ventricles. No chordae or papillary muscles; closure is passive.

Blood Flow Pathway:
Deoxygenated blood: SVC/IVC/Coronary sinus → Right Atrium → Tricuspid Valve → Right Ventricle → Pulmonary Valve → Pulmonary Artery → Lungs (oxygenation).
Oxygenated blood: Pulmonary Veins → Left Atrium → Mitral Valve → Left Ventricle → Aortic Valve → Aorta → Systemic Circulation.

Cardiac Skeleton: Fibrous rings (annuli fibrosi) surround the AV and semilunar valves, providing structural support, anchoring valve leaflets, and electrically insulating the atria from the ventricles (except at the AV node).

Heart Wall Layers:
Epicardium (visceral pericardium): Outer layer, contains coronary vessels and fat.
Myocardium: Thick muscular layer, arranged in spiraling fibers that enable wringing motion (ejection).
Endocardium: Inner endothelial lining, continuous with blood vessels.

Understanding heart structure is essential for clinical cardiology: valvular heart disease (stenosis—narrowing; regurgitation—leak), myocardial infarction (coronary artery occlusion damaging specific ventricular walls), heart failure (impaired pump function), congenital defects (septal defects, valve malformations), and surgical interventions (valve replacement, coronary bypass).

image.alt: Anterior view of the heart showing four chambers (right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle), great vessels (aorta, pulmonary trunk, SVC, IVC, pulmonary veins), and valves. Cutaway view showing internal structures: tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral, aortic valves; papillary muscles; chordae tendineae.
image.caption: Heart Structure: Four chambers (RA, RV, LA, LV), four valves (tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral, aortic), and major vessels. The right heart pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs; left heart pumps oxygenated blood to body.

sections.section_title: Introduction: The Heart as a Dual Pump
sections.introduction:
The heart is the central organ of the cardiovascular system, responsible for propelling blood through two circuits: the pulmonary circulation (to the lungs for oxygenation) and the systemic circulation (to the body for nutrient and gas exchange). Its structure is exquisitely adapted to this dual-pump function, with four chambers arranged in series, four valves ensuring unidirectional flow, and a specialized muscular wall (myocardium) that contracts rhythmically and efficiently.

Anatomically, the heart is located in the mediastinum, between the lungs, with its base (posterior surface) directed toward the right shoulder and its apex (tip) directed downward, leftward, and anteriorly. The apex is palpated as the point of maximal impulse (PMI) in the fifth intercostal space at the midclavicular line.

The heart is enclosed in the pericardium: fibrous pericardium (outer, tough) and serous pericardium (inner, with visceral epicardium and parietal layers). The pericardial cavity contains fluid that reduces friction during heartbeats.

The cardiac cycle is driven by coordinated contraction (systole) and relaxation (diastole). During diastole, ventricles relax, AV valves open, and ventricles fill with blood. During systole, ventricles contract, AV valves close (first heart sound, S1), and semilunar valves open, ejecting blood into the great arteries. When ventricles relax, semilunar valves close (second heart sound, S2).

Understanding heart structure is fundamental to clinical medicine: auscultation identifies valve sounds (location of murmurs), electrocardiography (ECG) detects electrical conduction, echocardiography visualizes chambers and valves, and cardiac catheterization assesses pressures and coronary anatomy. Cardiac surgery repairs or replaces diseased valves, bypasses occluded coronary arteries, and corrects congenital defects.

introduction_image.alt: Heart in thoracic cavity: orientation (apex leftward, base posterior), relationship to lungs, sternum, diaphragm. Pericardial layers: fibrous pericardium, serous pericardium (parietal, visceral/epicardium), pericardial cavity.
introduction_image.caption: Heart Orientation and Pericardium: Heart lies in mediastinum with apex directed leftward and downward. Pericardial layers reduce friction and provide structural support.

key_concept.definition: The heart has four chambers: right atrium and ventricle (pulmonary pump) and left atrium and ventricle (systemic pump), with four valves (tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral, aortic) ensuring unidirectional blood flow through the pulmonary and systemic circuits.
key_concept.context:
The right heart (low-pressure system) pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs; its thinner walls reflect the low resistance of the pulmonary circulation. The left heart (high-pressure system) pumps oxygenated blood to the body; its thicker walls (especially the left ventricle) generate the high pressures needed for systemic perfusion. The atria serve as receiving chambers and booster pumps (atrial kick), contributing 20-30% of ventricular filling. The ventricles are the primary pumping chambers.

Clinical relevance: Valvular heart disease (mitral stenosis—narrowing of mitral valve; aortic regurgitation—leakage of aortic valve; tricuspid stenosis, etc.) causes characteristic murmurs and can lead to heart failure. Septal defects (atrial septal defect—ASD; ventricular septal defect—VSD) cause shunting of blood between chambers, leading to volume overload. Myocardial infarction (heart attack) damages ventricular muscle, impairing pump function. Hypertrophy (thickening of ventricular wall) occurs in response to pressure overload (e.g., hypertension, aortic stenosis).

image.alt: Cutaway diagram of heart showing all four chambers, valves (tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral, aortic), papillary muscles, chordae tendineae, and direction of blood flow (arrows).
image.caption: Heart Chambers and Valves: Four chambers (RA, RV, LA, LV) and four valves ensure unidirectional blood flow. Papillary muscles and chordae tendineae prevent AV valve prolapse.

sections.key_concept: Each heart chamber and valve has specialized structural features adapted to its functional role; the cardiac skeleton provides structural support and electrical insulation; the myocardium is arranged in spiraling layers for efficient contraction.

classifications.value: Best for understanding the anatomy, pressure characteristics, and structural adaptations of each chamber.
characteristics: Right Atrium (RA): Thin-walled, low-pressure chamber. Receives deoxygenated blood from: superior vena cava (upper body), inferior vena cava (lower body), coronary sinus (myocardium). Contains: crista terminalis (smooth muscular ridge dividing smooth and trabeculated portions), pectinate muscles (muscular ridges in auricle), fossa ovalis (remnant of fetal foramen ovale). SA node located at junction of SVC and RA.

Right Ventricle (RV): Thinner wall (3-5 mm) than LV. Pumps blood into pulmonary trunk (low resistance, ~15-25 mmHg systolic pressure). Internal structures: trabeculae carneae (muscular ridges), moderator band (septomarginal trabecula) carrying right bundle branch, papillary muscles (anterior, posterior, septal) anchoring tricuspid valve via chordae tendineae. Inflow and outflow portions separated by supraventricular crest.

Left Atrium (LA): Thin-walled, forms most of heart base. Receives oxygenated blood from four pulmonary veins. Smooth-walled except for pectinate muscles in auricle. Contains valve of foramen ovale on septal wall.

Left Ventricle (LV): Thick-walled (8-15 mm, 3× thicker than RV) generating high pressure (120 mmHg systolic). Pumps blood into aorta. Internal structures: trabeculae carneae (fine), two papillary muscles (anterior, posterior) anchoring mitral valve via chordae tendineae. No moderator band. Shape is conical with narrower apex.
classifications.behavior:
Atrial function: Atria serve as reservoir (during ventricular systole), conduit (during early ventricular diastole), and booster pump (atrial systole, late diastole). The atrial kick contributes 20-30% of ventricular filling; loss of atrial contraction (atrial fibrillation) reduces cardiac output, especially in patients with diastolic dysfunction.

Ventricular function: Right ventricle is a low-pressure pump adapted for volume displacement. Its thinner wall and crescentic shape (in cross-section) allow it to eject blood efficiently against low resistance. Left ventricle is a high-pressure pump adapted for pressure generation. Its thicker wall and circular shape (in cross-section) generate high pressures for systemic circulation. Ventricular geometry: LV is conical; RV wraps around LV.

Clinical correlations: Right ventricular hypertrophy occurs in pulmonary hypertension, chronic lung disease (cor pulmonale). Left ventricular hypertrophy occurs in hypertension, aortic stenosis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Dilated cardiomyopathy involves chamber enlargement with reduced contractility. Atrial enlargement occurs in chronic pressure/volume overload (mitral stenosis → LA enlargement; pulmonary hypertension → RA enlargement).

examples.explanation:
Left ventricular hypertrophy is an adaptive response to chronic pressure overload: (1) Mechanism: Hypertension increases afterload (resistance against which LV must pump). To maintain wall stress (LaPlace's law: wall stress = (pressure × radius)/(2 × wall thickness)), the LV increases wall thickness (concentric hypertrophy). (2) Structural changes: Increased myocyte size, increased collagen deposition. (3) ECG findings: Increased QRS voltage (Sokolow-Lyon criteria: SV1 + RV5/6 >35 mm), ST-T wave changes (strain pattern). (4) Echocardiography: Increased LV wall thickness (≥11 mm), normal or small cavity size. (5) Consequences: Diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation), increased oxygen demand, risk of heart failure, arrhythmias, sudden death. (6) Treatment: Blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors, ARBs) can reverse hypertrophy.

Significance: LVH illustrates the heart's structural adaptation to altered hemodynamic load and the clinical importance of identifying hypertrophy as a marker of end-organ damage in hypertension.

image.alt: Echocardiography: normal LV vs. concentric LVH (thickened walls, small cavity). ECG: increased QRS voltage, ST-T strain pattern. LaPlace's law diagram: increased pressure → increased wall thickness to maintain stress.
image.caption: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH) in Hypertension: Chronic pressure overload causes concentric hypertrophy, increasing wall thickness to normalize wall stress. LVH is a marker of end-organ damage and risk factor for heart failure.

classifications.value: Best for understanding valve structure, mechanics, and the pathophysiology of valvular heart disease.
characteristics: Atrioventricular (AV) Valves: Tricuspid (right) and Mitral (left). Structure: Leaflets (cusps) of dense fibrous tissue covered by endocardium; chordae tendineae (fibrous cords) attach leaflets to papillary muscles; papillary muscles contract with ventricles, pulling chordae to prevent leaflet prolapse into atria. Mitral valve has two leaflets (anterior, posterior); tricuspid has three (anterior, posterior, septal). AV valves open during diastole (ventricular filling), close during systole (ventricular contraction).

Semilunar Valves: Pulmonary (right) and Aortic (left). Structure: Three cusps (semilunar shape); no chordae or papillary muscles; cusps are thinner than AV valve leaflets; have noduli (thickened central nodules) ensuring complete closure. Open during ventricular systole (ejection), close during diastole as blood in pulmonary trunk and aorta pushes cusps together.

Valve Function: (1) Competence: Valves prevent backflow (regurgitation) when closed. (2) Opening: During forward flow, pressure gradient forces valves open. (3) Closure: AV valves close when ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure; semilunar valves close when arterial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure.

Cardiac Skeleton: Fibrous rings (annuli fibrosi) surrounding each valve; provide structural support, anchor valve leaflets, and serve as attachment for myocardium; electrically insulate atria from ventricles (except at AV node).
classifications.behavior:
Valvular mechanics: AV valves close passively when ventricular pressure rises above atrial pressure during systole. Papillary muscle contraction, occurring simultaneously with ventricular contraction, tenses chordae tendineae, preventing leaflet prolapse into the atria. Semilunar valves open when ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure; they close passively when ventricular pressure falls below arterial pressure, with blood filling the cusps.

Valvular heart disease: Stenosis (narrowing) prevents full opening → increased pressure load on upstream chamber (e.g., aortic stenosis → LV pressure overload, concentric hypertrophy). Regurgitation (incompetence) allows backflow → volume overload on upstream chamber (e.g., mitral regurgitation → LA and LV volume overload, eccentric hypertrophy).

Clinical correlations: Mitral stenosis (most commonly rheumatic heart disease): thickened, fused mitral valve leaflets → impaired filling → LA enlargement, atrial fibrillation, pulmonary hypertension, right heart failure. Mitral regurgitation: incomplete mitral valve closure → systolic murmur, LV volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy → eventually LV dysfunction. Aortic stenosis: calcific degeneration of trileaflet or bicuspid valve → LV pressure overload → concentric hypertrophy, angina, syncope, heart failure. Aortic regurgitation: incompetent aortic valve → LV volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy, widened pulse pressure, bounding pulses. Infective endocarditis: bacterial infection of heart valves (especially damaged valves or prosthetic valves) → vegetations, valve destruction, systemic emboli.

examples.explanation:
Aortic stenosis (AS) is narrowing of the aortic valve, causing left ventricular pressure overload: (1) Etiology: Calcific degeneration (most common in elderly), bicuspid aortic valve (congenital), rheumatic heart disease. (2) Hemodynamics: LV must generate higher pressure to eject blood across narrowed valve → LV systolic pressure rises (up to 200-300 mmHg). (3) Adaptive response: Concentric LV hypertrophy (increased wall thickness) normalizes wall stress (LaPlace's law) but increases oxygen demand. (4) Clinical triad: Angina (impaired coronary flow reserve), syncope (exertional, due to fixed cardiac output), heart failure (dyspnea, orthopnea, pulmonary edema). (5) Examination: Harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur (radiates to carotids), delayed carotid upstroke (pulsus parvus et tardus), S4 gallop. (6) Imaging: Echocardiography—valve area <1.0 cm² (severe), peak velocity >4 m/s, mean gradient >40 mmHg. (7) Treatment: Aortic valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter—TAVR) for severe symptomatic AS; no effective medical therapy.

Significance: Aortic stenosis illustrates the consequences of chronic pressure overload on the left ventricle and the importance of valve replacement in symptomatic patients.

image.alt: Aortic stenosis: thickened, calcified aortic valve with narrowed orifice. Hemodynamics: increased LV pressure, increased wall thickness (concentric hypertrophy). Murmur location (right second intercostal space, radiating to carotids). Echocardiography: valve area, peak velocity.
image.caption: Aortic Stenosis: Calcific narrowing of aortic valve causes LV pressure overload, concentric hypertrophy, and clinical triad (angina, syncope, heart failure). Definitive treatment is valve replacement.

classifications.value: Best for understanding the arrangement of cardiac muscle fibers and its functional significance in contraction.
characteristics: Epicardium: Visceral pericardium; outer layer of heart; contains coronary arteries, veins, and adipose tissue; serous membrane with mesothelium.

Myocardium: Thick muscular layer; cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes) arranged in spiraling layers; intercalated discs (gap junctions, desmosomes) provide electrical and mechanical coupling. Fiber orientation: superficial fibers wrap around both ventricles; deeper fibers are arranged in spiraling bands that originate at fibrous rings, descend toward apex, and loop back. This arrangement enables a "wringing" motion during systole (contraction shortens and twists).
Endocardium: Inner endothelial lining; continuous with endothelium of blood vessels; thin layer of connective tissue and subendocardial layer containing Purkinje fibers (conduction system).
Pericardium: Fibrous pericardium (outer, tough) and serous pericardium (parietal and visceral layers). Pericardial cavity contains 15-50 mL of fluid for lubrication.
classifications.behavior:
Myocardial fiber orientation is critical for efficient ejection. The spiral arrangement means that contraction produces: (1) Shortening of the ventricles (longitudinal axis); (2) Constriction of the ventricular cavity (circumferential); (3) Twisting (wringing) motion, with apex rotating counterclockwise relative to base (viewed from apex). This twisting motion, known as ventricular torsion, contributes to efficient ejection and is diminished in heart failure.

The myocardium has the highest oxygen consumption per gram of any tissue, extracting 70-80% of oxygen from coronary blood (most organs extract 20-30%). Coronary blood flow occurs primarily during diastole, as systolic compression impedes flow.

Clinical correlations: Myocardial infarction (MI) results from coronary artery occlusion → ischemic necrosis of myocardium in a specific territory (e.g., left anterior descending artery occluded → anterior wall infarction; right coronary artery → inferior wall infarction). MI can lead to complications: pump failure (cardiogenic shock), arrhythmias, pericarditis, rupture, ventricular aneurysm. Cardiomyopathies: hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (thickened LV, often with outflow obstruction); dilated cardiomyopathy (enlarged, thin-walled, reduced contractility); restrictive cardiomyopathy (stiff myocardium, impaired filling).

examples.explanation:
Coronary artery occlusion causes myocardial infarction in a specific territory: (1) Left anterior descending (LAD) artery: Supplies anterior wall of LV, anterior septum, apex. Occlusion → anterior wall MI; ECG: ST elevation in V1-V4 leads; complications: pump failure, septal rupture. (2) Right coronary artery (RCA): Supplies inferior wall of LV, posterior septum, RV, SA node (60%), AV node (90%). Occlusion → inferior wall MI; ECG: ST elevation in II, III, aVF; complications: bradyarrhythmias (AV block), RV infarction (hypotension). (3) Left circumflex (LCx) artery: Supplies lateral wall of LV. Occlusion → lateral wall MI; ECG: ST elevation in I, aVL, V5-V6. (4) Myocardial layers: Transmural MI (full thickness) vs. subendocardial MI (inner layer). Subendocardium is most vulnerable to ischemia (least collateral flow, highest wall stress).

Significance: Understanding coronary anatomy and its relationship to ventricular territories enables rapid ECG localization of MI, guiding reperfusion therapy (PCI, thrombolytics) and risk stratification.

image.alt: Coronary arteries: LAD (anterior wall), RCA (inferior wall), LCx (lateral wall). ECG leads: V1-V4 (anterior), II/III/aVF (inferior), I/aVL/V5-V6 (lateral). Cross-section showing transmural vs. subendocardial infarction.
image.caption: Myocardial Infarction Territories: Coronary artery occlusion damages specific ventricular walls. ECG lead distribution localizes the infarct territory, guiding acute management.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of Heart Structure and Function

principles.principle: Unidirectional Blood Flow is Maintained by Valves and Pressure Gradients
principles.statement: Blood flows through the heart in one direction because AV valves open when atrial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure (diastole) and close when ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure (systole); semilunar valves open when ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure (systole) and close when arterial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure (diastole)—pressure gradients, not active muscular closure, drive valve function.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) During diastole: Ventricular pressure < atrial pressure → AV valves open; blood flows from atria to ventricles. (2) Atrial systole (late diastole): Atrial contraction increases atrial pressure, contributing additional filling (atrial kick). (3) Isovolumetric contraction: Ventricles contract; ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure → AV valves close (S1). Ventricular pressure < arterial pressure → semilunar valves remain closed. No ejection occurs. (4) Ejection: Ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure → semilunar valves open; blood ejected. (5) Isovolumetric relaxation: Ventricles relax; ventricular pressure < arterial pressure → semilunar valves close (S2). Ventricular pressure > atrial pressure → AV valves remain closed. No filling occurs. (6) When ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure, AV valves open, cycle repeats.

Clinical application: Valvular heart disease disrupts pressure gradients: stenosis requires higher pressure gradient to open valve; regurgitation allows backflow when valve should be closed. Heart sounds correspond to valve closures: S1 (AV valves close) marks beginning of systole; S2 (semilunar valves close) marks beginning of diastole. Extra heart sounds (S3, S4) reflect abnormal filling dynamics.
principles.example:
Mitral regurgitation: Incomplete mitral valve closure allows blood to leak from LV to LA during systole. (1) Pathophysiology: During LV systole, some blood flows retrograde into LA (in addition to forward flow into aorta). (2) Consequences: LA volume overload → LA enlargement; LV volume overload (must pump forward plus regurgitant volume) → eccentric LV hypertrophy → eventually LV dysfunction. (3) Hemodynamics: Regurgitant jet reduces forward stroke volume; compensatory mechanisms maintain cardiac output initially. (4) Examination: Holosystolic murmur (best heard at apex, radiating to axilla), S3 gallop (in severe cases). (5) Treatment: Valve repair (preferred) or replacement for severe symptomatic regurgitation or when LV dysfunction develops.

Significance: Mitral regurgitation illustrates the importance of valve competence in maintaining unidirectional flow and the compensatory adaptations (eccentric hypertrophy) that occur in response to volume overload.
principles.analogy: Heart valves are like one-way doors: They open when pressure pushes from behind, and close when pressure pushes from the front. AV valves are like doors between rooms (atria and ventricles); semilunar valves are like exit doors (out of the heart). The doors don't actively close—pressure differences do the work.

image.alt: Pressure tracings: atrial pressure, ventricular pressure, aortic pressure. Valve opening and closure points: AV valve open (diastole), AV valve close (S1), semilunar valve open (ejection), semilunar valve close (S2). Phases: filling, isovolumetric contraction, ejection, isovolumetric relaxation.
image.caption: Cardiac Cycle and Valve Function: Pressure gradients determine valve opening and closure. AV valves open when atrial pressure > ventricular pressure; close when ventricular pressure > atrial pressure. Semilunar valves open when ventricular pressure > arterial pressure; close when arterial pressure > ventricular pressure.

principles.statement: Ventricular wall thickness is proportional to the afterload (resistance against which the ventricle pumps): the left ventricle, pumping against high systemic resistance (mean arterial pressure ~90-100 mmHg), has a thick wall (8-15 mm); the right ventricle, pumping against low pulmonary resistance (~15-25 mmHg), has a thin wall (3-5 mm)—and chronic pressure overload induces concentric hypertrophy to normalize wall stress.
principles.application:
Functional inference: LaPlace's law for a sphere (or cylinder): wall stress (σ) = (P × r) / (2h), where P = pressure, r = radius, h = wall thickness. For a given pressure and radius, increasing wall thickness reduces wall stress. Chronic pressure overload (aortic stenosis, hypertension) → increased P → if no hypertrophy, wall stress increases → hypertrophy normalizes stress. Chronic volume overload (mitral regurgitation, aortic regurgitation) → increased r (dilation) → eccentric hypertrophy (increased h proportionally to maintain stress).

Clinical application: Echocardiography measures wall thickness and chamber size to identify hypertrophy (concentric vs. eccentric). Concentric hypertrophy (increased thickness, normal/small cavity) indicates pressure overload. Eccentric hypertrophy (increased thickness and cavity size) indicates volume overload. Right ventricular hypertrophy indicates pulmonary hypertension or pulmonary stenosis.
principles.example:
Pulmonary hypertension and right ventricular hypertrophy: (1) Etiology: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), chronic thromboembolic disease, left heart failure, chronic lung disease (COPD, interstitial lung disease). (2) Pathophysiology: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance → increased afterload on RV. (3) Adaptive response: RV concentric hypertrophy (increased wall thickness). However, RV has limited ability to tolerate pressure overload compared to LV; with prolonged pressure overload, RV dilates and fails (cor pulmonale). (4) Clinical: Right heart failure—jugular venous distension, hepatomegaly, peripheral edema, ascites. (5) Imaging: Echocardiography shows RV hypertrophy, RV dilation, elevated pulmonary artery pressure (estimated from tricuspid regurgitation jet). (6) Treatment: Address underlying cause; pulmonary vasodilators (for PAH: prostacyclins, endothelin antagonists, PDE5 inhibitors).

Significance: RV hypertrophy in pulmonary hypertension illustrates the principle that wall thickness reflects afterload, and that different ventricles have different capacities for pressure adaptation.
principles.analogy: Ventricular wall thickness is like the thickness of a pipe: A pipe carrying high-pressure water needs thick walls to avoid bursting; a pipe carrying low-pressure water can have thin walls. The heart's walls thicken in response to chronic pressure overload, like pipes thickening under high pressure.

image.alt: LaPlace's law diagram: sphere with pressure (P), radius (r), wall thickness (h), wall stress (σ = P×r/2h). Pressure overload → concentric hypertrophy (↑h); volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy (↑r, ↑h proportionally).
image.caption: LaPlace's Law: Wall stress = (Pressure × Radius) / (2 × Wall Thickness). Pressure overload causes concentric hypertrophy (increased thickness); volume overload causes eccentric hypertrophy (increased radius and thickness).

principles.principle: Cardiac Skeleton Electrically Insulates Atria from Ventricles
principles.statement: The fibrous cardiac skeleton (annuli fibrosi surrounding the valves, membranous septum, and fibrous trigones) provides structural support for the valves and serves as an electrical insulator, preventing direct spread of depolarization from atria to ventricles—forcing conduction through the AV node and His-Purkinje system, enabling coordinated, sequential contraction.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) The atria and ventricles are separated by fibrous tissue except at the AV node. (2) Without this insulation, electrical impulses would spread directly from atria to ventricles, causing simultaneous contraction (ventricular pre-excitation). (3) The AV node delays conduction (AV delay), allowing atrial contraction to complete before ventricular contraction. (4) The His-Purkinje system rapidly conducts impulses to all ventricular myocytes, ensuring coordinated contraction.

Clinical application: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome results from an accessory pathway (bundle of Kent) that bypasses the AV node, causing pre-excitation (delta wave on ECG, short PR interval). AV node ablation is used to treat refractory arrhythmias. Calcification of the cardiac skeleton (mitral annular calcification) can occur with aging and may interfere with valve function or conduction.
principles.example:
Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome: (1) Anatomy: Accessory pathway (bundle of Kent) connects atria to ventricles across the AV groove, bypassing the AV node. (2) Physiology: Impulse conducts rapidly via accessory pathway, depolarizing part of ventricle early (pre-excitation) before the normal impulse through AV node arrives. (3) ECG: Short PR interval (<120 ms), delta wave (slurred upstroke of QRS), wide QRS. (4) Arrhythmias: Re-entrant tachycardia (antegrade via AV node, retrograde via accessory pathway, or vice versa); risk of atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (can degenerate to ventricular fibrillation). (5) Treatment: Catheter ablation of accessory pathway (curative).

Significance: WPW syndrome illustrates the functional importance of the cardiac skeleton's electrical insulation and the consequences of accessory pathways that bypass this barrier.
principles.analogy: The cardiac skeleton is like a firewall between the atria and ventricles. The only connection is through the AV node (like a controlled gateway), ensuring that the electrical signal takes the proper path and that the ventricles contract only after the atria have finished filling.

image.alt: Cardiac skeleton: fibrous rings around valves, membranous septum. Normal conduction: SA node → atria → AV node (delay) → His-Purkinje → ventricles. WPW: accessory pathway bypasses AV node → pre-excitation (delta wave).
image.caption: Cardiac Skeleton and Conduction: Fibrous rings electrically insulate atria from ventricles, forcing conduction through the AV node. Accessory pathways (WPW) bypass this insulation, causing pre-excitation.

components.definition: Chambers: RA, RV, LA, LV. Valves: Tricuspid (3 leaflets), Pulmonary (3 cusps), Mitral (2 leaflets), Aortic (3 cusps). Great Vessels: SVC, IVC, pulmonary trunk, pulmonary veins, aorta. Cardiac Skeleton: annuli fibrosi, membranous septum. Myocardium: spiraling muscle fibers. Pericardium: fibrous and serous layers.
components.equation: Stroke Volume (SV) = End-Diastolic Volume (EDV) - End-Systolic Volume (ESV); Ejection Fraction (EF) = SV/EDV (normal 55-70%).
components.interpretation: Ejection fraction is the percentage of end-diastolic volume ejected with each beat, reflecting ventricular systolic function. Normal EF is 55-70%; EF <40% indicates systolic heart failure. EF is a key clinical parameter measured by echocardiography, used to classify heart failure (HFrEF: EF <40%; HFpEF: EF ≥50%).

image.alt: Labeled diagram of heart: chambers, valves, great vessels. Tables of normal dimensions: LV wall thickness, chamber sizes. Ejection fraction calculation (EDV, ESV).
image.caption: Key Components: Four chambers, four valves, great vessels. Ejection fraction (EF) measures systolic function; wall thickness and chamber size are assessed by echocardiography.

categories.type: Coronary Circulation: Arterial Supply and Venous Drainage
categories.description: The coronary arteries supply the myocardium; the right and left coronary arteries arise from the aortic sinuses (just above the aortic valve). The coronary venous system drains into the coronary sinus (into RA).
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Left Main Coronary Artery: Divides into: Left Anterior Descending (LAD)—anterior LV wall, anterior septum, apex; Left Circumflex (LCx)—lateral LV wall. Right Coronary Artery (RCA): Supplies inferior LV wall, posterior septum, RV, SA node (60%), AV node (90%). Coronary dominance: Right dominant (70%)—RCA supplies posterior descending artery (PDA); Left dominant (10-15%)—LCx supplies PDA; Codominant (15%).

Coronary Veins: Great cardiac vein (drains LAD territory) → coronary sinus → RA; middle cardiac vein (drains PDA territory) → coronary sinus; small cardiac vein (drains RCA territory) → coronary sinus. Anterior cardiac veins drain directly into RA.

examples.explanation:
Coronary artery bypass grafting is a surgical revascularization procedure: (1) Indications: Left main coronary artery stenosis >50%; three-vessel disease; two-vessel disease with proximal LAD involvement; failed medical therapy; acute coronary syndromes. (2) Conduits: Left internal mammary artery (LIMA) to LAD (gold standard, 10-year patency >90%); saphenous vein grafts (to RCA, LCx, diagonal, obtuse marginal branches); radial artery grafts. (3) Procedure: Cardiopulmonary bypass (on-pump) or beating heart (off-pump); graft anastomosed distal to stenosis. (4) Outcomes: Improved survival in left main and three-vessel disease; symptom relief. (5) Complications: Graft failure (vein grafts have 50% patency at 10 years), perioperative MI, stroke, bleeding, infection.

Significance: CABG is based on detailed knowledge of coronary anatomy—targeting specific vessels for grafting—and has been shown to improve survival in patients with severe coronary artery disease.

image.alt: Coronary artery anatomy: LAD, LCx, RCA. CABG: LIMA to LAD; saphenous vein grafts to RCA and LCx. Graft patency rates. Surgical approach.
image.caption: Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting (CABG): Conduits (LIMA, saphenous vein) bypass coronary stenoses. LIMA to LAD has superior long-term patency.

categories.type: Fetal Circulation and Transition at Birth
categories.description: Fetal circulation includes structures that bypass the non-functioning fetal lungs: foramen ovale (between atria), ductus arteriosus (between pulmonary artery and aorta), ductus venosus (bypasses liver). At birth, these structures close, establishing postnatal circulation.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Fetal circulation: Oxygenated blood from placenta → umbilical vein → ductus venosus (bypasses liver) → IVC → RA → most blood directed through foramen ovale to LA → LV → aorta → systemic circulation. Deoxygenated blood from SVC → RA → RV → pulmonary artery → ductus arteriosus (bypasses lungs) → descending aorta → umbilical arteries → placenta.

Transition at birth: (1) First breath increases pulmonary blood flow; (2) Increased LA pressure (from pulmonary venous return) closes foramen ovale (fossa ovalis). (3) Increased oxygen tension causes ductus arteriosus constriction (functional closure in hours, anatomical closure in weeks). (4) Ductus venosus constricts.

Clinical: Patent foramen ovale (PFO)—25% of adults have probe-patent foramen; associated with cryptogenic stroke (paradoxical embolism). Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA)—common in preterm infants; left-to-right shunt; treated with indomethacin (constricts ductus) or surgical/transcatheter closure.

examples.explanation:
PDA is persistence of the fetal ductus arteriosus after birth: (1) Pathophysiology: Ductus fails to close → left-to-right shunt (aorta to pulmonary artery) due to higher systemic pressure. (2) Hemodynamics: Increased pulmonary blood flow → pulmonary overcirculation; increased return to LA/LV → volume overload; continuous (systolic and diastolic) flow across PDA. (3) Clinical: Continuous "machinery" murmur (best heard at left infraclavicular area); wide pulse pressure; bounding pulses. (4) Complications: Left heart volume overload → heart failure; pulmonary hypertension → Eisenmenger syndrome (reversal to right-to-left shunt with cyanosis). (5) Treatment: Indomethacin (prostaglandin inhibitor) in preterm infants; transcatheter occlusion (coil or device) or surgical ligation in older children and adults.

Significance: PDA illustrates the transition from fetal to postnatal circulation and the clinical consequences of failure of ductal closure.

image.alt: Fetal ductus arteriosus; postnatal PDA with left-to-right shunt; continuous murmur; echocardiography showing ductal flow; transcatheter closure device.
image.caption: Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA): Failure of ductus arteriosus closure after birth causes left-to-right shunt (aorta to pulmonary artery), volume overload, and continuous murmur.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Heart Structure Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Heart Structure Case Analysis: Valvular Heart Disease
worked_examples.problem: A 68-year-old woman presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion (now limited to one flight of stairs), orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. She has a history of hypertension. Examination: blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, heart rate 88 bpm, respiratory rate 20. Auscultation: crisp S1, S2 with a loud S4 gallop; a late-peaking crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the right second intercostal space radiating to the carotid arteries; diminished carotid upstroke. (1) Which valve is affected? (2) What is the likely lesion (stenosis or regurgitation)? (3) What structural changes are present in the left ventricle? (4) What are the treatment options?

step_by_step.explanation: Localize the murmur to a specific valve.
step_by_step.calculation: Murmur location: right second intercostal space (aortic area) → aortic valve. Radiation to carotids → aortic valve. Timing: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur → ejection murmur (occurs during systole, between S1 and S2). Systolic ejection murmur at aortic area → aortic stenosis or aortic sclerosis. Diminished carotid upstroke (pulsus parvus et tardus) → consistent with aortic stenosis.

Systolic murmur → stenosis (obstruction to outflow). Aortic regurgitation produces a diastolic decrescendo murmur. The loud S4 (atrial gallop) indicates reduced ventricular compliance (diastolic dysfunction), common in pressure overload states like aortic stenosis. Symptoms (dyspnea, orthopnea, PND) indicate heart failure due to aortic stenosis.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify structural changes in the left ventricle.
step_by_step.calculation: Aortic stenosis causes pressure overload → left ventricular concentric hypertrophy (increased wall thickness, normal or small cavity). Hypertrophy increases oxygen demand and causes diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation → elevated filling pressures → S4). Prolonged pressure overload may lead to systolic dysfunction (dilated LV, reduced EF).

Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis is an indication for aortic valve replacement (AVR). Options: Surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) or transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR). TAVR is preferred in elderly patients (≥75 years) or high-risk surgical candidates. No effective medical therapy for severe AS; vasodilators are contraindicated (can cause hypotension).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Affected valve: Aortic valve. (2) Lesion: Aortic stenosis (narrowing of aortic valve, causing systolic ejection murmur). (3) Left ventricular structural changes: Concentric hypertrophy (increased wall thickness) due to chronic pressure overload; diastolic dysfunction (S4 gallop). (4) Treatment: Aortic valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter—TAVR) for severe symptomatic aortic stenosis.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding valve anatomy and function, the cardiac cycle, and the relationship between valve lesions and ventricular remodeling enables accurate localization of the pathology from physical examination and guides treatment selection.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Blood Flow Through the Heart
sections.introduction: Blood flows through the heart in a continuous cycle, driven by pressure gradients and regulated by valves. This flow illustrates the sequential filling and ejection of the four chambers.

process_flow.description: Deoxygenated blood returns from the body via the superior vena cava (SVC), inferior vena cava (IVC), and coronary sinus to the right atrium.
process_flow.mechanism: SVC drains upper body (head, neck, arms, upper chest). IVC drains lower body (abdomen, pelvis, legs). Coronary sinus drains the myocardium. Blood enters RA during ventricular systole and diastole; RA pressure is low (0-8 mmHg).

image.alt: SVC, IVC, coronary sinus delivering deoxygenated blood to right atrium.
image.caption: Step 1: Venous Return. Deoxygenated blood from the body enters the right atrium via SVC, IVC, and coronary sinus.

process_flow.process: Right Atrial Contraction and Ventricular Filling
process_flow.description: During diastole, the tricuspid valve opens, and blood flows from RA to RV. Atrial systole (late diastole) contributes additional filling (atrial kick).
process_flow.mechanism: Ventricular pressure < atrial pressure → tricuspid valve opens. Passive filling (early diastole) accounts for 70-80% of ventricular volume. Atrial contraction (atrial kick) adds 20-30%.

image.alt: Blood flow from right atrium through tricuspid valve into right ventricle during diastole. Atrial contraction shown.
image.caption: Step 2: Right Ventricular Filling. Tricuspid valve opens; blood flows from RA to RV. Atrial contraction (atrial kick) contributes additional filling.

process_flow.description: During systole, RV contracts, tricuspid valve closes, pulmonary valve opens, and blood is ejected into the pulmonary trunk.
process_flow.mechanism: RV pressure exceeds RA pressure → tricuspid valve closes (S1). RV pressure exceeds pulmonary artery pressure → pulmonary valve opens → blood ejected into pulmonary trunk → pulmonary arteries → lungs.
process_flow.result: Deoxygenated blood delivered to lungs for oxygenation

image.alt: Right ventricle contracts; tricuspid valve closes; pulmonary valve opens; blood ejected into pulmonary trunk and pulmonary arteries.
image.caption: Step 3: Right Ventricular Ejection. RV contraction closes tricuspid valve (S1) and opens pulmonary valve, ejecting deoxygenated blood to the lungs.

process_flow.process: Pulmonary Venous Return to Left Atrium
process_flow.description: Oxygenated blood returns from the lungs via four pulmonary veins to the left atrium.
process_flow.mechanism: Gas exchange occurs in pulmonary capillaries; oxygenated blood returns via pulmonary veins to LA. LA pressure is low (0-12 mmHg).

image.alt: Oxygenated blood from lungs returns via pulmonary veins to left atrium.
image.caption: Step 4: Pulmonary Venous Return. Oxygenated blood from the lungs enters the left atrium via the four pulmonary veins.

process_flow.process: Left Atrial Contraction and Ventricular Filling
process_flow.description: During diastole, the mitral valve opens, and blood flows from LA to LV. Atrial systole contributes the atrial kick.
process_flow.mechanism: LV pressure < LA pressure → mitral valve opens. Passive filling (early diastole) followed by atrial contraction. Mitral valve has two leaflets (anterior, posterior) and chordae tendineae attached to papillary muscles.

image.alt: Blood flow from left atrium through mitral valve into left ventricle during diastole.
image.caption: Step 5: Left Ventricular Filling. Mitral valve opens; oxygenated blood flows from LA to LV. Atrial contraction contributes the atrial kick.

process_flow.description: During systole, LV contracts, mitral valve closes, aortic valve opens, and blood is ejected into the aorta.
process_flow.mechanism: LV pressure exceeds LA pressure → mitral valve closes (S1). LV pressure exceeds aortic pressure → aortic valve opens → blood ejected into aorta → systemic circulation. Aortic valve has three cusps and no chordae.
process_flow.result: Oxygenated blood delivered to systemic circulation

image.alt: Left ventricle contracts; mitral valve closes; aortic valve opens; blood ejected into aorta for systemic distribution.
image.caption: Step 6: Left Ventricular Ejection. LV contraction closes mitral valve (S1) and opens aortic valve, ejecting oxygenated blood into the systemic circulation.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Developmental Anatomy and Congenital Heart Disease
sections.definition: The heart develops from a primitive heart tube that loops and septates to form four chambers. Congenital heart defects (CHDs) result from failure of normal development—ranging from simple defects (ASD, VSD) to complex anomalies (tetralogy of Fallot, transposition of the great arteries).

process_types.energy_profile: The primitive heart tube (week 3) undergoes rightward looping (week 4) and septation (weeks 4-7) to form the four-chambered heart.
process_types.direction: Development: Cardiogenic mesoderm → paired heart tubes fuse → primitive heart tube (truncus arteriosus, bulbus cordis, ventricle, atrium, sinus venosus). Looping: Heart tube bends rightward → establishes future chambers. Septation: Endocardial cushions form AV septum and valves; atrial septum forms (septum primum, septum secundum); ventricular septum forms (muscular and membranous parts); truncus arteriosus divided by conotruncal ridges into aorta and pulmonary trunk.
process_types.function: Understand normal cardiac development; explain the basis of congenital heart defects; guide prenatal diagnosis and postnatal management.
goals: Explain mechanisms of congenital heart defects
Apply to clinical diagnosis and management

primary_example.process: Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF): Most Common Cyanotic Congenital Heart Defect
primary_example.explanation:
Tetralogy of Fallot results from anterior malalignment of the conotruncal septum: (1) Four components: Ventricular septal defect (VSD), overriding aorta (aorta overrides VSD), right ventricular outflow tract obstruction (pulmonary stenosis), right ventricular hypertrophy. (2) Embryology: Failure of conotruncal ridges to spiral correctly → anterior displacement of conotruncal septum → unequal division of truncus → small pulmonary trunk and large aorta → VSD. (3) Physiology: RV outflow obstruction → right-to-left shunt across VSD → cyanosis. (4) Clinical: Cyanosis at birth or progressive; "tet spells" (hypercyanotic episodes) with crying or exercise; squatting increases systemic vascular resistance, reducing right-to-left shunt. (5) Examination: Harsh systolic ejection murmur at left sternal border (pulmonary stenosis); single S2 (absent pulmonary component). (6) Imaging: Echocardiography shows anatomy; chest X-ray shows "boot-shaped" heart (upturned apex, concave pulmonary artery). (7) Treatment: Surgical repair (patch closure of VSD, relief of RV outflow obstruction) in infancy; palliative Blalock-Taussig shunt in symptomatic infants prior to complete repair.

Significance: Tetralogy of Fallot illustrates how failure of normal cardiac septation (conotruncal development) produces a constellation of structural abnormalities that together determine the clinical presentation and surgical approach.

image.alt: Tetralogy of Fallot: VSD, overriding aorta, pulmonary stenosis, RV hypertrophy. Embryology: conotruncal septal malalignment. Clinical: boot-shaped heart, tet spells. Surgical repair.
image.caption: Tetralogy of Fallot: Cyanotic congenital heart disease with four components. Results from abnormal conotruncal septation. Surgical repair corrects the anatomy.

image.alt: Embryonic heart development: primitive heart tube → looping → septation → four-chambered heart. Timeline: weeks 3-8.
image.caption: Cardiac Development: The heart develops from a primitive tube that loops and septates to form four chambers. Congenital heart defects result from failure of normal septation.

process_types.energy_profile: Modern cardiac imaging modalities (echocardiography, cardiac CT, cardiac MRI) provide detailed visualization of heart chambers, valves, and function.
process_types.direction: Echocardiography (TTE, TEE): Ultrasound imaging of heart structure and function; measures chamber sizes, wall thickness, valve morphology, ejection fraction, and Doppler flow. Cardiac CT: High-resolution imaging for coronary arteries, valve anatomy, congenital heart disease. Cardiac MRI: Gold standard for volumes, function, and tissue characterization (fibrosis, infiltration).
process_types.function: Diagnose structural heart disease; guide intervention (valve replacement, coronary stenting); monitor disease progression.
goals: Understand imaging modalities for heart structure

primary_example.explanation:
Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) uses standard views to visualize heart structures: (1) Parasternal long-axis: LV, LA, aortic valve, mitral valve, RV outflow tract. (2) Parasternal short-axis: LV at base (aortic valve), mid (papillary muscles), apex; RV. (3) Apical four-chamber: All four chambers, septa, AV valves. (4) Subcostal: IVC, atrial septum. (5) Doppler: Color flow identifies regurgitation, stenosis, shunts; spectral Doppler measures velocities (gradients). (6) Measurements: LV ejection fraction (Simpson's biplane), LV wall thickness, chamber sizes, valve areas (continuity equation for aortic stenosis), pulmonary artery pressure (tricuspid regurgitation velocity).

Significance: Echocardiography is the primary imaging modality for heart structure, providing real-time, non-invasive assessment of anatomy and function.

image.alt: Standard echocardiographic views: parasternal long-axis (LV, LA, aortic valve, mitral valve); apical four-chamber (all four chambers); parasternal short-axis at base (aortic valve).
image.caption: Echocardiography: Standard views visualize heart chambers, valves, and function. Doppler assesses flow velocities and pressure gradients.

image.alt: Cardiac imaging modalities: TTE (ultrasound), cardiac CT (coronary arteries), cardiac MRI (myocardial tissue, volumes).
image.caption: Cardiac Imaging: Echocardiography (structure, function), cardiac CT (coronaries, valve anatomy), cardiac MRI (volumes, tissue characterization) provide complementary information.

sections.relationship: Heart structure is the anatomical foundation for cardiovascular function. The four-chamber design with unidirectional valves enables efficient separation of pulmonary and systemic circulations. The cardiac skeleton provides structural support and electrical insulation. The myocardial fiber orientation enables efficient contraction. Understanding normal heart structure is essential for recognizing abnormalities in congenital heart disease, valvular pathology, and myocardial disease. Advanced imaging technologies now allow detailed visualization of heart structure, guiding surgical and transcatheter interventions.

summary:
The heart is a four-chambered dual pump: right heart (RA, RV) pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs; left heart (LA, LV) pumps oxygenated blood to the body. Four valves ensure unidirectional flow: tricuspid (right AV), pulmonary (right semilunar), mitral (left AV), aortic (left semilunar).

Blood flow pathway: SVC/IVC/coronary sinus → RA → tricuspid valve → RV → pulmonary valve → pulmonary artery → lungs (oxygenation) → pulmonary veins → LA → mitral valve → LV → aortic valve → aorta → systemic circulation.

Chamber adaptations: LV is thick-walled (8-15 mm) to generate high systemic pressure; RV is thin-walled (3-5 mm) for low-pressure pulmonary circulation. Pressure overload (aortic stenosis, hypertension) causes concentric hypertrophy (increased wall thickness); volume overload (regurgitation) causes eccentric hypertrophy (dilation).

Valve structure: AV valves have leaflets, chordae tendineae, and papillary muscles that prevent prolapse. Semilunar valves have three cusps and close passively. Valvular heart disease (stenosis—narrowing; regurgitation—leakage) causes characteristic murmurs and leads to chamber remodeling.

Cardiac skeleton: Fibrous rings surround valves, providing structural support and electrically insulating atria from ventricles (except at AV node). Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome results from accessory pathways that bypass this insulation.

Myocardial architecture: Spiraling muscle fibers enable a "wringing" contraction (ventricular torsion) that efficiently ejects blood. Coronary arteries (LAD, LCx, RCA) supply the myocardium; occlusion causes myocardial infarction in specific territories.

Fetal circulation: Foramen ovale (atrial shunt) and ductus arteriosus (aortopulmonary shunt) bypass the non-functioning fetal lungs. At birth, these close; failure of closure causes patent foramen ovale (PFO) or patent ductus arteriosus (PDA).

Congenital heart defects result from abnormal development: tetralogy of Fallot (VSD, overriding aorta, pulmonary stenosis, RV hypertrophy) is the most common cyanotic defect.

Understanding heart structure—chambers, valves, myocardium, and vessels—is essential for interpreting physical examination (murmurs, gallops), echocardiography (chamber sizes, valve function), and guiding treatment of valvular disease, coronary artery disease, and heart failure.
